summary: Paul Graham argues that the most reliable way to get rich is by creating wealth through startups, which compress a lifetime of work into a few intense years where measurement and leverage are highest. He distinguishes wealth from money, rejects the “fixed pie” fallacy, and explains why small teams tackling hard technical problems can create outsized value. The essay also stresses the role of luck and risk in outcomes, and why user demand is the ultimate test of value.
key_points: Startups let individuals trade time and intensity for high upside by combining measurement and leverage.
Wealth is what people want, money is just the medium of exchange; creating wealth is not zero-sum.
Small, selective teams outperform large organizations because individual contributions are more measurable.
Technology provides leverage because a new technique scales to many users, unlike labor-bound services.
Luck and variance dominate outcomes; most startups fail despite high productivity.
money_quotes: Startups are not magic.  They don't change the laws of wealth creation.
Wealth is what you want, not money.
To get rich you need to get yourself in a situation with two things, measurement and leverage.
If you have two choices, choose the harder.
key_insight: The fastest path to wealth is to create something people want in a small, measurable team with technological leverage, accepting high risk and variance.
